u1x6iEOZLy4.title: Reading Chitonga Lesson 3 Simple Method
u1x6iEOZLy4.prompt_used: This is a Chitonga (Tonga) language lesson. The instructor teaches reading, writing, and vocabulary in Chitonga. Chitonga vowels: a e i o u. Consonant-vowel pairs are taught. Common Chitonga: ndakuyan...

segments.text: Hello everyone. In this video, I will be showing you how to read Chitonga.
There is only one rule that you need to follow in order to help you read Chitonga.
The rule is divide the word where a vowel is followed by a consonant or a different vowel.
So this is just to help you read out the parts of the word individually and then you piece them together to read the whole word.
So examples. The first word is Chitonga. So you divide where a vowel is followed by a consonant.
So between I and T, you divide. Between O and N, you divide.
So CI has the sound JI. TO has the sound TO. NGA has the sound NGA. Together it makes JITONGA.
You do the same for the second word. You divide where a vowel follows a consonant.
So you divide between A and M. You divide again between U and L. You also divide where there is A and G.
So A has the sound A. MU has the sound MU. LYA has the sound LYA. NGO has the sound NGO.
That is MU, LYA, NGO.
More examples. The first word is Goiliha. So you divide where a vowel is followed by a different vowel.
That is between O and I. You also divide where a vowel follows a consonant between I and L. So divide again between I and Z.
KO has the sound KO. I has the sound I. LI has the sound LI. ZYA has the sound ZYA.
The following word is Rwimbo. So you divide where a vowel is followed by a different vowel or a consonant.
So here I and I are the same so you leave them together. Then you divide between I and M.
This LW double I is LWI. So you make the sound B longer because of the double I. So you say LWI instead of just LWI.
So you say LWI. If it was just a single I it would have been LWI. Now since it is two I's it is LWI.
So for each part, after you divide the word, any word that you can divide the Tonga word, I have made the reference for the sounds. I think I have covered about 99% of the sounds.
So you just have to check in the playlist. There is a playlist. I will link it in the description where you can find the sounds for each part.
That's all for this lesson. Thank you. Subscribe and like the video.

j6SQyi7SCfU.title: How to say Come here in Chitonga
j6SQyi7SCfU.prompt_used: This is a Chitonga (Tonga) language lesson. The instructor teaches reading, writing, and vocabulary in Chitonga. Chitonga vowels: a e i o u. Consonant-vowel pairs are taught. Common Chitonga: ndakuyan...

segments.text: How to say come here in Chitonga. Boola kuno. Boola kuno. An example of this would be, let's say you're trying to call John, so you say, John, come here. In Chitonga, you'd say, John, boola kuno.
In Chitonga, you'd say, John, boola kuno.
